topic: A person walking across the Abbey Road zebra crossing, eye-level view.

parsed_generation.narration: Shot 1 shows a single person entering the Abbey Road zebra crossing from the near edge, filmed at eye level to place the viewer on equal footing with the subject. Shot 2 follows as the person moves across the crosswalk, with a slow eye-level tracking in medium framing to keep the diagonal stripes and the crossing rhythm visible. Shot 3 ends as the person reaches the far side, held in a close eye-level frame to complete the crossing moment. The sequence keeps one subject in frame and anchors the action in the iconic setting, emphasizing the simple, human act of crossing.

feedback: The scene stays centered on a single person crossing the Abbey Road zebra crossing with eye-level framing, but the gold standard favors a leaner, two-shot structure focusing on establishing the crossing and then the action of stepping onto the stripes. The narration adds some context, but could more tightly frame the iconic setting and visual cues (diagonal stripes, cars stopped in the background). To align, condense to two shots and emphasize the precise action of entering and crossing the crosswalk with concise, cinematic language.
strengths: Maintains eye-level perspective and Abbey Road setting.
Keeps a single-subject focus throughout the sequence.
fixes: Reduce the sequence to two shots to mirror the gold standard and clearly separate establishment from action (static establishing shot, then crossing action on the stripes).

Adopt crisper, concise cinematic phrasing (e.g., 'static eye-level wide of the crossing' then 'step onto the white stripes and walk toward the center'), emphasizing the iconic elements (diagonal lines, cars stopped).
